channel.title: NYT > Arts > Art & Design
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Final, Flying Colors of Matisse’s ‘Second Life’
item.description: A breathtaking Paris show challenges the conventional idea that artists taper off at the end of their lives.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge

item.title: Brooklyn Museum’s African Art Galleries to Unite Continent
item.description: With the planned creation of new galleries for its Arts of Africa collection, the Brooklyn Museum hopes visitors will see their cultures “represented with dignity.”
item.dc:creator: Aruna D’Souza

item.title: New Museum Debuts New Building With Ambitious ‘New Humans’
item.description: It’s a big, serious, adult show worth debating and even fighting over — just the way our critic likes it.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago and Elliott Jerome Brown Jr.

item.title: Why Felix Gonzalez-Torres’s Go-Go Dancer Piece Remains Subversive
item.description: A work about gay visibility avoids statements, yet remains powerful. A dancer appears just once a day, showing the political valence of absence.
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl and Marcus Maddox
item.media:credit: Marcus Maddox for The New York Times

item.title: Christopher Columbus Statue Is Installed on White House Grounds
item.description: The statue of the explorer, a replica of one that protesters toppled in 2020, was placed outside the Eisenhower Executive Office Building.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: A statue of Christopher Columbus was installed overnight in front of the Eisenhower Executive Office Building in Washington on Sunday.

item.title: A Painter Faces His Biggest Show, and the Truth About Success
item.description: As Hurvin Anderson prepares for a major retrospective of his work at Tate Britain, he’s unsure how to feel about his achievements.
item.dc:creator: Kadish Morris and Tom Jamieson For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Tom Jamieson for The New York Times

item.title: At the 34th Outsider Art Fair, Still Genuine Surprises
item.description: Cosmic explosions, proto-Surrealism and names to remember — like the D.J. Raul Hardie and Anne Brown, the high point of our critic’s survey.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Dana Golan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Works by the Gullah artist Sam Doyle at the Gallery of Everything Booth at the Outsider Art Fair in Manhattan. Using house paint on found tin and board, Doyle captured his subjects with graphic confidence.

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in March
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Dawn Chan covers Yuko Mohri’s flood abatements, Jackie Saccoccio’s evocative drips, Raquel Rabinovich’s luscious mud and Josiane M.H. Pozi’s anti-“likes.”
item.dc:creator: Dawn Chan, Max Lakin and Seph Rodney
item.category: American Academy of Arts and Letters

item.title: Through Bamboo, the Artist Lap-See Lam Explores Her Family’s History
item.description: In a homecoming of sorts, Lap-See Lam has brought her multidisciplinary works to Hong Kong for her first solo show in Asia.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth

item.title: Console Tables That Fit In Anywhere
item.description: Sculptural lines and mixed materials make these workhorse pieces both practical and cool.

item.title: Wicked Stepmother No Longer, a Female Pharoah Gets a Reputational Makeover
item.description: A reassessment of damaged 3,500-year-old statuary adds to evidence that Queen Hatshepsut wasn’t the villain that scholars long took her to be.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: How to Reduce the Environmental Impact of Collecting
item.description: From flying commercial to choosing reusable packing crates, art enthusiasts have many options to consider
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: Gold, A New Art Space in Hong Kong, Opens
item.description: Recalling the era of Picasso and Hemingway, the location in Hong Kong, called Gold, will host art, design and fashion exhibitions, and more.
item.dc:creator: Dean Napolitano

item.title: A Painter Reveals Hong Kong’s Natural Wonders — and Where to See Them
item.description: Growing up, Stephen Wong Chun Hei did not get outside much. Now, he hikes the city’s trails and renders them in wild color. Here are six of his favorites.
item.dc:creator: Anjali Tsui

item.title: Outside Art Basel Hong Kong, Cyborgs, Abstractions and More
item.description: The city’s museums and galleries entice with work by boldfaced names like Lee Bul and Mary Weatherford, plus shows on textiles and collective memory.
item.dc:creator: Mercedes Hutton
item.media:credit: Lee Bul via M+, Hong Kong
item.media:description: Works by Lee Bul on display at M+. The exhibition includes pieces that explore the aspirations (and often follies) of utopian endeavors, along with works from her seminal “Cyborg” series.

item.title: Exploring Hong Kong’s Textile Trail
item.description: At the peak, Hong Kong was one of Asia’s largest textile exporters. Now, visitors can see tangible reminders of that history in several neighborhoods.
item.dc:creator: Christy Choi

item.title: Art Basel Hong Kong: Injecting Energy Into the Art Fair Experience
item.description: A four-person team has shaped the Encounters section of Art Basel Hong Kong, organizing it around the elements of water, fire, earth and ether.
item.dc:creator: Bonnie Tsui

item.title: At Hong Kong Art Basel, an Exhibition of Works by El Anatsui
item.description: The artist El Anatsui has returned to his roots in Ghana and is showing in Hong Kong during Art Basel.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: A Navy Veteran Finds His Niche in the Art World
item.description: It took courage for Walter Price to move to New York and pursue a career as a painter, but he says his dream has come true.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Oliver Farshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Contemporary painter Walter Price at his studio in Bushwick, Brooklyn.

item.title: Man Arrested After Valuable Glass Artwork Is Shattered at Seattle Museum
item.description: A man was arrested after being accused of damaging an estimated $240,000 of artwork at the Chihuly Garden and Glass.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.category: Chihuly Garden and Glass (Seattle, Wash, Museum)
item.media:credit: Chihuly Studio/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York; Photo: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chihuly Garden and Glass in Seattle. Twelve items were reported damaged, each valued at an estimated $20,000.

item.title: Calvin Tomkins, Who Profiled Giants of Modern Art, Dies at 100
item.description: On the staff of The New Yorker for more than 60 years, he wrote about Duchamp, Rauschenberg and many others. His books include “Living Well Is the Best Revenge.”
item.dc:creator: William Grimes
item.media:credit: Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
item.media:description: Calvin Tomkins at his home in Manhattan. A longtime staff writer at The New Yorker, he specialized in writing profiles of artists.

item.title: 9 Art Shows to Catch Before They Close This Spring
item.description: Among the must-see exhibitions on view for a limited time are ones featuring a rare Caravaggio, streetscapes covered in orchids and Gabriele Münter’s colorful figures.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.category: Museum of the City of New York
Pioneer Works Center for Art and Innovation (Brooklyn, NY)
item.media:credit: Succession Wifredo Lam, Adagp, Paris/ARS, NY; Guarionex Rodriguez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wifredo Lam’s “La Jungla (The Jungle)” (1942-43), which is on view at the Museum of Modern Art through April 11 in the retrospective “When I Don’t Sleep, I Dream.”

item.title: Thomas Gentille, Artist Who Made Wearable Sculpture, Dies at 89
item.description: He was a master jeweler, but his pieces looked more like miniature contemporary artworks than anything you’d find at Cartier.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:description: Thomas Gentille in 1980. “I think you can express everything in jewelry,” he said. “And that’s what I’m trying to do.”

item.title: Mending Ceramics and Slowing Time With the Japanese Art of Kintsugi
item.description: Plus: a guide to Honolulu’s Kaimuki neighborhood, a ski-in, ski-out guesthouse in Austria and more recommendations from T Magazine.

item.title: On a Majorcan Estate, a Collage Made Over Hundreds of Summer Holidays
item.description: A descendant of one of the island’s oldest families safeguards the history of her 18th-century home — which includes a sprawling feat of decoupage.
item.dc:creator: Zoey Poll and Ricardo Labougle

item.title: An English Countryside Home That Became Lovelier the More It Fell Apart
item.description: The ravages of time have only increased the appeal of one family’s art-filled manor in the English countryside.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Farrell and Henry Bourne

item.title: Inside Giancarlo Valle and Jane Keltner de Valle’s New York Townhouse
item.description: The overhaul of this seven-story home provided a husband-and-wife design duo with plenty of space to express their old-meets-new aesthetic.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Brazilian and Christopher Sturman

item.title: Inside Dorian Grinspan’s Upstate Home, Where Japanese Handicraft Meets ‘British Whimsy’
item.description: A film producer invited artists and friends to design his house with him, each adding ideas of their own.
item.dc:creator: Jason Chen and Juliana Sohn

item.title: Ida Ekblad’s Experimental Space Where Artists Come to Play
item.description: Ida Ekblad has transformed a concrete fortress into an experimental space for herself, and for others.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Farrell and Inger Marie Grini

item.title: Let Your Home Be
item.description: For some, the beauty of a space comes from allowing a space to go ungroomed.
item.dc:creator: Hanya Yanagihara

item.title: Trump’s Planning for America’s 250th Emphasizes Religion’s Role in the Nation’s Founding
item.description: A closed-door White House event included news about the National Garden of American Heroes and an emphasis on the role of religion in the founding.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler and Zachary Small

item.title: Got an Idea About Who Robbed the Gardner Museum? Get in Line.
item.description: Theories abound as to who pulled off the largest art heist in U.S. history. In a new book, the former F.B.I. agent who handled the case dismisses many of them.
item.dc:creator: Tom Mashberg
